notes: The demand indices (Alpha, Q0, Pmax, Breakpoint) were used in inferential analyses but not directly reported, although they plotted the aggregated/mean demand curve; only median R2 was reported, not mean.

"The Exponential Demand Equation provided an excellent fit to the overall demand data (R2=0.99) and a very good fit to the individual data (median R2 =0.81, interquartile range=0.74–0.85)."

I reported the overall R2 as the mean R2.

"Exponential equation provided a satisfactory fit to individual participants responses
(n=4790, mean R2 0.79, median 0.80)."

I indicated Yes to individual analyses based on this.

"To generate an estimate of Elasticity, demand curves were estimated by fitting each participant's reported consumption across the range of prices to Hursh and Silberberg's (2008) exponential demand curve equation"
The study featured two HPTs. Only the quantity HPT was reported here. Median R2 was reported, not mean.

demand_model: Hursh et al. (1988) exponential demand equation
notes:
"The demand curve equation was a good fit to the median
consumption data, with an R2 value of 0.98.  
For the majority of the individual participants, the demand curve equation also provided a good fit to the data, with a median R2 of 0.94 across participants. Of the 74 participants, only eight had R2 values that
were lower than 0.85. The median observed value of demand
intensity was 65.0 units of cocaine (interquartile range=20.0
145.5), and the median curve-calculated value (l) was 24.1
units (interquartile range=13.2–64.7). The median observed Pmax was $8.95 (interquartile range 2.50–25.00), and the
median curve-calculated Pmax was $12.52 (interquartile range
5.73–25.73)."
Observed and derived values were reported for most of the demand indices. I reported the derived values here.

population: Clinical sample: cocaine abuse or dependence

notes: Paper has two samples (cocaine disorder N=23; noncocaine control N=11), same commodity, not a multi-commodity case. Only cocaine group entered for sample size and mean age. Mean Alpha/Mean Q0: not reported as text values, only shown in bar graphs (Figure 2) with no numeric values given.
The reported Mean R2 is the model fit for data at the first timepoint.

This study conducted two experiments with two different HPTs on the same commodity. Each experiment had 4 versions of its respective HPT, and indices for all 8 versions were reported. 
So in this row and the next, I recorded the indices for the first version of both HPTs.

So in this row and the next, I recorded the indices for the first version of both HPTs.
The study reported indices of two demand curves, generated from the data collected at two different points (a longitudinal study). I recorded the indices of the "first wave."

Study has two groups (High-TWD n=25, Low-TWD n=24) crossed with two delay conditions (15-min, 60-min); group split is the primary study variable, so no overall/combined value exists for any index. All Section H fields left NR; values available only per group×condition cell (Pg 230, Fig. 1& Fig. 2).

journal: Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior
notes: The study reports both empirical and derived Q0. I reported the derived Q0.

The paper reports means for the demand indices derived from the CPT across 4 different conditions. I reported the indices for the first here. 

Neither individual nor aggregate analysis was stated explicitly but I interpreted the indices reported for the 4 different conditions as indicative of aggregate analysis.

notes:
The study reported an aggregate R² and a median individual R², rather than a mean.

Participant assignment was not explicitly reported for the Alcohol Purchase Task. Instead, the study reports that participants were randomized to one of two intervention conditions, but does not specify participant assignment for the Alcohol Purchase Task (See. pg 228, methods section)

demand_model: Other (Hursh & Winger’s 1995 demand curve normalization equation)
other_index: Omax (maximum expenditure); Intensity (consumption at zero cost)
notes:
Population: The population was framed as "Human Subjects Research Pool at the State University of New York at Binghamton" (pg. 1600). closest codebook match is college student, but not explicitly stated, so entered with [?] instead of blank.
Alpha: A parameter "a" (pg. 1601, Data analysis section) was used but not directly labeled alpha, since the study used the Hursh & Winger (1995) equation. Unsure if this parameter should count as alpha.
Conditions: Two conditions reported (neutral, alcohol cue). Used alcohol cue for mean breakpoint (higher drinking/demand). Used normalized Pmax alcohol condition over observed Pmax (clearer effect of cue). Used neutral for R² (0.94 vs. 0.93 - better fit). (See. pg 1602, Results & Table 2)

Q0/Alpha: Paper reports Intensity and Elasticity, not Q0/Alpha. Matched based on definitions only. (pg. 4)
Two samples, one commodity: Paper has two samples (gen. pop. N=369; clinical N=39), same commodity, not a multi-commodity case. Only gen. pop. entered. (pg. 5–6, 11)
Commodity Domain: "Mental health" is closest fit but may be weak; paper frames this as hypersexuality/impulse-control, not mental health per se. (pg. 3)
The means of the demand indices were reported in the paper, so I marked them 'yes.'

Paper has two samples (dependent N=19; nondependent N=15), same commodity, not a multi-commodity case. Only dependent group entered for sample size and mean age. Mean Alpha/Mean Q0 not calculated (elasticity not modeled, small N). Breakpoint/Pmax reported as medians only, not means.

Study tested three commodities (heroin, cocaine, benzodiazepines), entered as three separate rows. Q0, alpha, and EV entered are Aggregate only, since no individual-level average was reported for these three (only group-curve values, pg. 4); Mean R² instead uses the individual-level average (0.89/0.90/0.94) since that was explicitly reported (pg. 5).

Sample Size: N was not explicitly reported per drug/index in the paper. Derived personally from Table 2's F-test degrees of freedom (N = df2 + 2). (See pg. 5, Table 2)
Mean Age: Study reports age at both baseline (48.2) and follow-up (50.0) due to longitudinal design. Used baseline (T1) value for consistency with T1 demand indices reported in Section H. (See pg. 3, Table 1)
Same paper/sample as Schwartz2021-her - see row 30AL for full notes on commodity split, Aggregate labeling, sample size derivation, and age selection (all apply identically here)
Same paper/sample as Schwartz2021-her - see row 30AL for full notes on commodity split, Aggregate labeling, sample size derivation, and age selection (all apply identically here)

journal: Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior
other_index: Omax (maximum expenditure); observed intensity (raw consumption at lowest price)
notes: Three conditions reported (10%, 50%, 90% STI risk). Used 90% risk for Q0, breakpoint, Pmax, and alpha (clearest effect of STI risk on protective demand). Used raw R² (0.94) over normalized (0.88), consistent with raw values used above. (See pg. 5, Results & Table 2)
